title: Java Classes and Objects

examples.label: Define and instantiate a class

title: Concrete Class in Java
body:
A **concrete class** is a fully implemented class — it provides implementations for all its methods and can be instantiated directly with `new`.

This contrasts with:
- **Abstract classes** — cannot be instantiated; may have unimplemented methods
- **Interfaces** — define contracts without implementation (before Java 8 default methods)

A concrete class may extend an abstract class or implement interfaces, but it must provide a body for every abstract method it inherits.

Most everyday classes — `String`, `ArrayList`, `HashMap` — are concrete classes. When designing a system, prefer depending on interfaces or abstract types so you can swap concrete implementations without changing client code.

examples.label: Concrete class implementing an interface

title: Access Modifiers in Java
body:
Access modifiers control the visibility of classes, fields, and methods.

| Modifier | Class | Package | Subclass | World |
|----------|-------|---------|----------|-------|
| `public` | yes | yes | yes | yes |
| `protected` | yes | yes | yes | no |
| *(none)* | yes | yes | no | no |
| `private` | yes | no | no | no |

**Best practice:** use the most restrictive modifier possible.
- Fields: almost always `private`
- Helper methods: `private`
- API methods: `public`
- Members intended only for subclasses: `protected`

Access modifiers enforce encapsulation — a core OOP principle that hides implementation details from the outside world.

examples.label: Access modifier usage

title: A Guide to Constructors in Java
body:
A **constructor** is a special method called when an object is created. It initializes the object's state.

Key rules:
- Same name as the class
- No return type (not even `void`)
- Can be overloaded (multiple constructors with different parameters)

**Default constructor:** If you declare no constructors, Java provides a no-arg constructor automatically. As soon as you declare any constructor, the default is removed.

**Constructor chaining:** Use `this(...)` to call another constructor in the same class, or `super(...)` to call the parent constructor. These must be the first statement in the constructor body.

examples.label: Constructor overloading

title: A Guide to Creating Objects in Java
body:
Objects in Java can be created in several ways:

1. **`new` keyword** — most common: `Dog d = new Dog();`
2. **Reflection** — `Class.forName("Dog").getDeclaredConstructor().newInstance();`
3. **`clone()`** — shallow copy of an existing object (implement `Cloneable`)
4. **Deserialization** — reconstructing an object from a byte stream
5. **Factory methods** — static methods like `LocalDate.of(2024, 1, 1)` return instances

When `new` is used, the JVM:
1. Allocates memory on the heap
2. Calls the constructor
3. Returns a reference to the object

Object references are stored on the stack; the actual object data lives on the heap.

examples.label: Creating objects with new and factory method

title: Abstract Classes in Java
body:
An **abstract class** cannot be instantiated and may contain abstract methods — methods without a body that subclasses must implement.

```java
public abstract class Animal {
    abstract void speak();       // must be overridden
    void breathe() { ... }      // concrete — inherited as-is
}
```

Use an abstract class when:
- You want to share code among related classes
- Some behavior is common, some varies per subclass
- You want to define a template (Template Method pattern)

**Abstract vs Interface:**
- Abstract class can have state (fields) and constructors
- A class can extend only one abstract class
- Interfaces support multiple inheritance

examples.label: Abstract class with template method

title: Java Interfaces
body:
An **interface** defines a contract — a set of methods that implementing classes must provide. Interfaces support multiple inheritance: a class can implement many interfaces.

Since Java 8, interfaces may have:
- `default` methods — concrete methods with a body
- `static` methods — utility methods on the interface itself

Since Java 9: `private` methods in interfaces for code reuse within default methods.

```java
interface Drawable {
    void draw();                          // abstract
    default void print() { draw(); }      // default
    static Drawable noop() { return () -> {}; }
}
```

Choose an interface over an abstract class when you need to define a role that unrelated classes can adopt.

examples.label: Interface with default method

title: Guide to Inheritance in Java
body:
**Inheritance** lets a subclass acquire the fields and methods of its superclass using the `extends` keyword. Java supports single inheritance for classes.

The subclass:
- Inherits all non-private members of the parent
- Can add new fields and methods
- Can override parent methods with `@Override`
- Can call parent methods via `super.method()`

Constructor chaining: the first line of a subclass constructor implicitly calls `super()` unless you explicitly call `super(args)`. Always call `super(...)` explicitly when the parent has no no-arg constructor.

Inheritance models an "is-a" relationship: a `Dog` *is-a* `Animal`.

examples.label: Basic inheritance

title: Inheritance and Composition (Is-a vs Has-a) in Java
body:
**Inheritance** models an *is-a* relationship: a `Cat` is an `Animal`. Use it when the subclass truly specializes the parent.

**Composition** models a *has-a* relationship: a `Car` has an `Engine`. The class holds a reference to another class rather than extending it.

**Prefer composition over inheritance** because:
- Composition is more flexible — you can swap the component at runtime
- Inheritance creates tight coupling — changes to the parent break subclasses
- Deep inheritance hierarchies are hard to understand

Use inheritance when:
- The relationship is genuinely "is-a"
- You need polymorphism through a common base type
- The hierarchy is shallow (1-2 levels)

examples.label: Composition vs inheritance

title: Method Overloading and Overriding in Java
body:
**Overloading** — same method name, different parameter list, resolved at **compile time**:
```java
void print(int n) { ... }
void print(String s) { ... }
```

**Overriding** — subclass redefines a parent method with the same signature, resolved at **runtime** (dynamic dispatch):
```java
@Override
void speak() { System.out.println("Woof!"); }
```

Rules for overriding:
- Same method name and parameter types
- Return type must be the same or a covariant subtype
- Cannot reduce visibility (`public` method cannot become `protected`)
- Use `@Override` to catch mistakes at compile time

examples.label: Overloading and overriding

title: Composition, Aggregation, and Association in Java
body:
These three terms describe how objects relate to each other:

**Association** — objects know about each other but exist independently. A `Teacher` teaches `Students`, but both can exist separately.

**Aggregation** (weak has-a) — one object contains another, but the contained object can exist independently. A `Department` has `Employees`, but employees exist outside the department.

**Composition** (strong has-a) — the contained object's lifetime is tied to the container. A `House` has `Rooms`; destroying the house destroys the rooms.

In code, all three look like field references. The distinction is semantic — it matters for design and documentation, not syntax.

examples.label: Composition vs aggregation

title: Guide to the this Keyword in Java
body:
The `this` keyword refers to the current object — the instance on which a method or constructor is being called.

Common uses:

1. **Disambiguate fields from parameters:**
```java
this.name = name;
```

2. **Call another constructor in the same class:**
```java
this(0, 0); // must be first statement
```

3. **Pass the current object as an argument:**
```java
register(this);
```

4. **Return the current object (fluent API / builder pattern):**
```java
public Builder setName(String n) { this.name = n; return this; }
```

`this` cannot be used in `static` contexts because static methods have no associated instance.

examples.label: Builder pattern with this

title: Guide to the super Keyword in Java

examples.label: super constructor and method

title: A Guide to the Static Keyword in Java
body:
The `static` keyword makes a member belong to the **class** rather than any instance.

**Static fields** — shared across all instances:
```java
static int instanceCount = 0;
```

**Static methods** — callable without an instance:
```java
Math.abs(-5);
```

**Static blocks** — run once when the class is loaded:
```java
static { System.out.println("Class loaded"); }
```

**Static nested classes** — can be instantiated without an outer class instance.

Limitations of static:
- Cannot access instance fields/methods directly
- Cannot use `this` or `super`
- Harder to mock/test (prefer instance methods for business logic)

examples.label: Static counter

title: The final Keyword in Java
body:
The `final` keyword has three uses in Java:

1. **Final variable** — can be assigned only once (effectively a constant):
```java
final int MAX = 100;
```

2. **Final method** — cannot be overridden by subclasses:
```java
public final void template() { ... }
```

3. **Final class** — cannot be subclassed:
```java
public final class String { ... }
```

`String`, `Integer`, and other wrapper classes are `final` for security and immutability guarantees.

Using `final` communicates intent, enables compiler optimizations, and prevents accidental modification or extension. In combination with `private` fields and no setters, `final` fields are the key ingredient in immutable classes.

examples.label: final field and class

title: Java Record Keyword
body:
Introduced in Java 16, a **record** is a concise syntax for immutable data-holder classes. The compiler auto-generates:
- Private final fields
- A canonical constructor
- `equals()`, `hashCode()`, `toString()`
- Public accessor methods (field name, no `get` prefix)

```java
record Point(int x, int y) {}
Point p = new Point(3, 4);
System.out.println(p.x()); // 3
```

Records can have:
- Custom constructors (compact or explicit)
- Instance methods
- Static fields and methods

Records cannot extend other classes (they implicitly extend `java.lang.Record`) and cannot be mutable.

examples.label: Record with validation

title: A Guide to Java Enums
body:
An **enum** (enumeration) defines a fixed set of named constants. Enums in Java are full classes — they can have fields, constructors, and methods.

```java
enum Day { MON, TUE, WED, THU, FRI, SAT, SUN }
```

Enums are type-safe alternatives to integer constants. Useful features:
- `values()` — returns an array of all constants
- `ordinal()` — zero-based position
- `name()` — constant name as a String
- `Enum.valueOf(String)` — parse from string

Enums can implement interfaces, enabling polymorphic behavior per constant via abstract methods. Enums are implicitly `public static final` and cannot be instantiated with `new`.

examples.label: Enum with fields and methods

title: Guide To Java Optional
body:
`Optional<T>` is a container that may or may not hold a non-null value. It was introduced in Java 8 to reduce `NullPointerException` bugs by making the absence of a value explicit.

Creating an `Optional`:
```java
Optional.of(value)        // throws if null
Optional.ofNullable(val)  // wraps null as empty
Optional.empty()          // empty Optional
```

Using an `Optional`:
- `isPresent()` / `isEmpty()` — check
- `get()` — retrieve (throws if empty)
- `orElse(default)` — safe retrieval
- `orElseGet(supplier)` — lazy default
- `ifPresent(consumer)` — act if present
- `map()` / `flatMap()` / `filter()` — transform

Best practice: use `Optional` as a return type, not as a field or parameter.

examples.label: Optional usage

title: Comparing Objects in Java
body:
Java has two ways to compare objects:

**`==`** — compares **references** (memory addresses). Two references are `==` only if they point to the exact same object.

**`equals()`** — compares **logical equality** as defined by the class. `String.equals()` compares character content.

The default `equals()` inherited from `Object` uses `==`. Override it to define meaningful equality.

For ordering, implement `Comparable<T>` with `compareTo()`, or use external `Comparator<T>` objects.

Key rule: if you override `equals()`, you must also override `hashCode()` to maintain the contract that equal objects have equal hash codes.

examples.label: equals vs ==

title: Java equals() and hashCode() Contracts
body:
The **equals-hashCode contract** requires that:
- If `a.equals(b)` is `true`, then `a.hashCode() == b.hashCode()`
- If `a.hashCode() != b.hashCode()`, then `a.equals(b)` must be `false`
- The reverse is not required: equal hash codes do not imply equality (collision)

`equals()` must be: reflexive, symmetric, transitive, consistent, and return `false` for `null`.

Violating the contract breaks `HashMap`, `HashSet`, and any hash-based collection — objects stored with one hash code cannot be found if the code changes.

Best practice: generate `equals()` and `hashCode()` using `Objects.equals()` and `Objects.hash()` or use IDE generation / Lombok `@EqualsAndHashCode`.

examples.label: Correct equals and hashCode

title: Functional Interfaces in Java
body:
A **functional interface** has exactly one abstract method and can be represented as a lambda expression. The `@FunctionalInterface` annotation enforces this at compile time.

Built-in functional interfaces in `java.util.function`:

| Interface | Signature | Use |
|-----------|-----------|-----|
| `Supplier<T>` | `T get()` | produce a value |
| `Consumer<T>` | `void accept(T)` | consume a value |
| `Function<T,R>` | `R apply(T)` | transform |
| `Predicate<T>` | `boolean test(T)` | filter/test |
| `BiFunction<T,U,R>` | `R apply(T,U)` | two-arg transform |

Functional interfaces enable lambdas and method references throughout the Java API.

examples.label: Lambda as functional interface

title: Sealed Classes and Interfaces in Java
body:
**Sealed classes** (Java 17) restrict which classes can extend or implement them, giving you a closed, exhaustive type hierarchy.

```java
public sealed interface Result<T>
    permits Success, Failure {}

record Success<T>(T value) implements Result<T> {}
record Failure<T>(String error) implements Result<T> {}
```

Permitted subclasses must be in the same package (or module) and must be `final`, `sealed`, or `non-sealed`.

Benefits:
- Compiler-verified exhaustive switch
- Documents the intended hierarchy
- Replaces fragile `instanceof` chains
- Enables algebraic data types (sum types) in Java

examples.label: Sealed hierarchy with switch

title: Functional Programming in Java
body:
Functional programming (FP) treats computation as the evaluation of pure functions and avoids shared mutable state.

Java supports FP through:
- **Lambda expressions** — anonymous functions
- **Method references** — `String::toUpperCase`
- **Functional interfaces** — `Function`, `Predicate`, `Supplier`, `Consumer`
- **Streams** — declarative data pipelines
- **Immutable data** — records, `Collections.unmodifiableList()`

**Core FP principles in Java:**
- **Pure functions** — no side effects, same output for same input
- **Higher-order functions** — methods that accept or return functions
- **Function composition** — `f.andThen(g)`, `f.compose(g)`
- **Immutability** — prefer `final` and records

examples.label: Function composition

title: The transient Keyword in Java
body:
The `transient` keyword marks a field that should be **excluded from serialization**. When an object is serialized (converted to a byte stream), transient fields are not included and are set to their default values upon deserialization.

```java
class User implements Serializable {
    String username;
    transient String password; // never serialized
}
```

Common uses for `transient`:
- Sensitive data (passwords, credit card numbers)
- Derived/computed fields that can be recalculated
- Non-serializable fields (sockets, threads, streams)
- Cached values

Alternatively, override `writeObject()` and `readObject()` for custom serialization logic.

examples.label: transient field

title: Java instanceof Operator

title: How to Make a Deep Copy of an Object in Java
body:
A **shallow copy** copies an object's field values — but for reference fields, it copies the reference, not the referenced object. Changes to the nested object affect both copies.

A **deep copy** recursively copies all nested objects, producing a fully independent clone.

Approaches to deep copy in Java:
1. **Manual constructor** — copy each field explicitly
2. **`clone()` with `Cloneable`** — error-prone, deprecated by many
3. **Serialization** — serialize and deserialize (slow)
4. **Copy libraries** — Gson/Jackson round-trip, Apache Commons `SerializationUtils`

Records are immutable, so shallow copy is sufficient — this is one advantage of using records for data.

examples.label: Manual deep copy

title: Object Type Casting in Java
body:
**Type casting** converts an object reference from one type to another.

**Upcasting** (implicit) — subclass to superclass. Always safe:
```java
Animal a = new Dog(); // Dog → Animal
```

**Downcasting** (explicit) — superclass to subclass. May throw `ClassCastException`:
```java
Dog d = (Dog) a; // may fail at runtime
```

Always use `instanceof` before downcasting, or use pattern matching:
```java
if (a instanceof Dog d) { d.bark(); }
```

Primitive casting follows different rules:
- Widening (int → long → double) is implicit
- Narrowing (double → int) is explicit and may lose data

examples.label: Safe downcast with instanceof

title: Static and Default Methods in Interfaces in Java
body:
Before Java 8, interfaces could only have abstract methods. Java 8 added:

**Default methods** — provide a default implementation that implementing classes inherit or override:
```java
default void log(String msg) { System.out.println(msg); }
```

**Static methods** — utility methods on the interface, not inherited by implementing classes:
```java
static Validator of(Predicate<String> p) { return p::test; }
```

Java 9 added **private methods** in interfaces for code reuse within default methods.

These features allow interfaces to evolve without breaking existing implementations — crucial for the Java Collections API update in Java 8 (e.g., `List.forEach()`).

examples.label: Interface with default and static methods

title: Guide to Java Reflection
body:
**Reflection** allows a program to inspect and manipulate classes, methods, and fields at runtime — even private members — without knowing them at compile time.

Core classes in `java.lang.reflect`:
- `Class<?>` — represents a class or interface
- `Method` — represents a method
- `Field` — represents a field
- `Constructor<?>` — represents a constructor

Common operations:
```java
Class<?> clazz = obj.getClass();
Method m = clazz.getMethod("methodName", ParamType.class);
m.invoke(obj, args);
```

Uses: dependency injection frameworks (Spring), ORM (Hibernate), testing libraries, serialization. Reflection is powerful but slow and bypasses compile-time safety — use sparingly.

examples.label: Inspect a class with reflection
